coredata.dc:title: Enhanced vision-transformer integrating with semi-supervised transfer learning for state of health and remaining useful life estimation of lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ya-Xiong
Zhao, Shangyu
Wang, Shiquan
Ou, Kai
Zhang, Jiujun
coredata.dc:description: The state of health (SOH) and remaining useful life (RUL) of lithium-ion batteries are crucial for health management and diagnosis. However, most data-driven estimation methods heavily rely on scarce labeled data, while traditional transfer learning faces challenges in handling domain shifts across various battery types. This paper proposes an enhanced vision-transformer integrating with semi-supervised transfer learning for SOH and RUL estimation of lithium-ion batteries. A depth-wise separable convolutional vision-transformer is developed to extract local aging details with depth-wise convolutions and establishes global dependencies between aging information using multi-head attention. Maximum mean discrepancy is employed to initially reduce the distribution difference between the source and target domains, providing a superior starting point for fine-tuning the target domain model. Subsequently, the abundant aging data of the same type as the target battery are labeled through semi-supervised learning, compensating for the source model's limitations in capturing target battery aging characteristics. Consistency regularization incorporates the cross-entropy between predictions with and without adversarial perturbations into the gradient backpropagation of the overall model. In particular, across the experimental groups 13–15 for different types of batteries, the root mean square error of SOH estimation was less than 0.66 %, and the mean relative error of RUL estimation was 3.86 %. Leveraging extensive unlabeled aging data, the proposed method could achieve accurate estimation of SOH and RUL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: State of health (SOH)
Remaining useful life (RUL)
Depth-wise separable convolutional vision-transformer
Transfer learning
Maximum mean discrepancy
Semi-supervised learning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Depth-wise separable convolutional vision-transformer for SOH and RUL estimation
Semi-supervised transfer learning with consistency regularization
Battery datasets description and features extraction
Verification of MMD-based transfer learning for the same type of batteries
Verification of semi-supervised transfer learning with consistency regularization for different types of batteries
Validation results of different battery experimental groups
Validation results with different labeling rates in target domain
Validation results within different SOC windows

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic framework of the proposed method for SOH and RUL estimation.
Working mechanism of depth-wise separable convolutional vision transformer.
Working mechanism of depth-wise separable convolution.
Aging features extraction of B5 battery from NASA: (a) Incremental capacity curve; (b) Charging voltage; (c) Number of cycles; (d) Charging duration.
Validation results of experimental groups 1–3: (a) results from B7; (b) errors from B7; (c) results from B6; (d) errors from B6; (e) results from B5; (f) errors from B5.
Validation results of experimental groups 4–6: (a) results from B7; (b) errors from B7; (c) results from B6; (d) errors from B6; (e) results from B5; (f) errors from B5.
Validation results of experimental group 7–9: (a) results from B7; (b) errors from B7; (c) results from B6; (d) errors from B6; (e) results from B5; (f) errors from B5.
Validation results of experimental groups 10–12: (a) results from CS2_35; (b) errors from CS2_35; (c) results from CS2_36; (d) errors from CS2_36; (e) results from CS2_37; (f) errors from CS2_37.
Validation results of experimental groups 13–15: (a) results from Cell1; (b) errors from Cell1; (c) results from Cell2; (d) errors from Cell2; (e) results from Cell3; (f) errors from Cell3.
Validation results of experimental groups 16–17: (a) results from Lab1; (b) errors from Lab 1; (c) results from Lab2; (d) errors from Lab2.
Validation results with different labeling rates: (a) estimation results; (b) regression results; (c) estimation error; (d) error distribution.
Validation results with different SOC windows: (a) estimation results; (b) regression results; (c) estimation error; (d) error distribution.
Implementation procedure of the proposed method.

entry.bold: Algorithm: semi-supervised transfer learning based on enhanced vision-transformer

row.entry: target domain model for SOH estimation.
Initialization: initialize the DeViT model weights θ and normalize the input data.
Hyperparameters setting: epoch, batch size, learning rate, convolution kernel, etc.

entry.italic: total_loss = λ × regression_loss + β × mmd_loss+ ψ × consistency_loss

row.entry: Backpropagate total_loss and update model parameters θ
Train the source domain model with Ds by following steps 4-7 and MMD.
Freeze multi-head attention weights and transfer the model to the target domain.
Fine-tune the model using the updated target domain sample Dt ' by following steps 4-7.

head.ce:title: Enhanced vision-transformer integrating with semi-supervised transfer learning for state of health and remaining useful life estimation of lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering and Automation, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical Engineering and Automation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Mechanical Engineering and Automation, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The state of health (SOH) and remaining useful life (RUL) of lithium-ion batteries are crucial for health management and diagnosis. However, most data-driven estimation methods heavily rely on scarce labeled data, while traditional transfer learning faces challenges in handling domain shifts across various battery types. This paper proposes an enhanced vision-transformer integrating with semi-supervised transfer learning for SOH and RUL estimation of lithium-ion batteries. A depth-wise separable convolutional vision-transformer is developed to extract local aging details with depth-wise convolutions and establishes global dependencies between aging information using multi-head attention. Maximum mean discrepancy is employed to initially reduce the distribution difference between the source and target domains, providing a superior starting point for fine-tuning the target domain model. Subsequently, the abundant aging data of the same type as the target battery are labeled through semi-supervised learning, compensating for the source model's limitations in capturing target battery aging characteristics. Consistency regularization incorporates the cross-entropy between predictions with and without adversarial perturbations into the gradient backpropagation of the overall model. In particular, across the experimental groups 13–15 for different types of batteries, the root mean square error of SOH estimation was less than 0.66 %, and the mean relative error of RUL estimation was 3.86 %. Leveraging extensive unlabeled aging data, the proposed method could achieve accurate estimation of SOH and RUL.

ce:section.ce:para: In the context of energy shortage, lithium-ion batteries have been developed rapidly and used widely in recent decades due to their high energy density, long cycle life and high safety [1–3]. Effective and reliable battery management is necessary to ensure efficient and safe battery operation [4,5]. As the number of cycles of lithium-ion batteries increases, the reduction of the active ionic component of the battery, the proliferation of the solid electrolyte film, and the precipitation of insoluble substances in the electrolyte will continue to occur [6–8], which inevitably leads to the aging of the battery. Battery aging is generally described by the state of health (SOH) and remaining useful life (RUL) [9], so accurate SOH and RUL estimation is important for rationally formulating battery management strategies and ensuring the safety of battery operation [10]. However, once a lithium-ion battery is installed, its capacity cannot be calibrated in real time, and the nonlinear variations within the battery caused by operating conditions increase the difficulty of SOH and RUL estimation [11].

The SOH of a lithium-ion battery generally refers to the proportion of its maximum usable capacity compared to its nominal capacity. The RUL can be obtained by estimating the remaining cycle number based on the SOH at the end-of-life. Therefore, the estimation of SOH and RUL is often done concurrently [12], and the accuracy of RUL is directly influenced by the precision of the estimated SOH [13]. The methods for SOH estimation are usually categorized into three groups. The first category is based on laboratory measurements. Capacity calibration, internal resistance measurements, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy are commonly used for offline estimation of SOH with high accuracy [14–16]. However, the complexity of such methods and the need for precise test equipment hinder their popularization in practical applications [17]. The second class of methods is based on battery modeling [18]. Electrochemical models, equivalent circuit models, or fusion models are often combined with filtering algorithms for SOH estimation, and it is evident that this type of estimation method heavily relies on how accurately the battery is modeled. The internal mechanism of battery aging is so complex that it is difficult to be accurately described by existing battery models [19]. Moreover, accurately updating battery models at different aging stages remains a great challenge [20]. The third category of methods is based on data-driven model. Due to the rapid advancements in big data technology, artificial intelligence offers innovative solutions for estimating the SOH and RUL of lithium-ion batteries [21]. These approaches leverage machine learning and feature mining to establish a correlation model between data and target tasks, thus avoiding the consideration of intricate nonlinear dynamics within the battery [22]. Feature engineering and estimation model building constitute the mainstream of current research in data-driven SOH estimation. Incremental capacity (IC) and differential voltage (DV) curves are commonly used for aging features extraction [23]. Xiong et al. [24] extracted 568 aging features from measured data, IC, and DV curves, and combined four data analysis algorithms to develop a guiding algorithm for important features extraction. The estimation accuracy of the proposed method was improved by 63.5 % and 71.1 % on lithium-ion ternary and Li-Fe-phosphate batteries, respectively. The battery degradation prediction model aims to learn the correlation between features and aging behavior, facilitating further extraction of relevant features. Lee et al. [25] developed a machine learning framework for evaluating RUL prediction models based on seven artificial neural networks, which tuned the hyperparameters of each artificial neural network to the optimum, and achieved an estimation with mean absolute error (MAE) of 10.7 % using only 40 cycles of training data.

Although data-driven SOH and RUL estimation methods have made impressive progress, most of the data-driven models are built based on a fully supervised framework. Such methods extremely depend on a large amount of high-quality labeled data, which demands greater costs and time investments to acquire [26]. In real industrial scenarios, there are often only a small number of samples for training models, which undoubtedly poses difficulties for further research on battery degradation. Two types of learning paradigms, transfer learning and semi-supervised learning, have been investigated in recent years to solve such problems. Transfer learning typically leverages abundant data to train the source domain models and applies the pre-trained models to target domain with similar tasks, alleviating the requirement for large amounts of labeled data [27]. Deep transfer learning can be primarily categorized into four types: instance-based, mapping-based, network-based, and adversarial-based methods [28]. Mapping-based deep transfer learning maps data from two different domains into a common data space, while the network-based transfer learning pre-trains deep neural networks in the source domain and fine-tunes them in the target domain. These two types of transfer learning have been extensively studied and used in lithium-ion battery state estimation. Hofmann et al. [29] combined temporal convolutional network–long short term memory (LSTM) with transfer learning to address the dependency of open-circuit voltage reconstruction and SOH estimation models on extensive experimental data. The proposed method achieved SOH estimation with a mean absolute percentage error of less than 2.2 % under varying rate and voltage windows. Kim et al. [30] pre-trained LSTMs and simplified the process of data acquisition and training, enabling rapid adaptation to environmental changes and achieving SOH estimation with MAE and root mean square error (RMSE) less than 1 %. To further extend the application of transfer learning, Couture et al. [31] provided new insights into fast RUL estimation using transfer learning techniques. A novel feature extraction method for charge/discharge cycling images was developed to reduce the data preprocessing workload and bias, combined with transfer learning to achieve fast extraction of battery aging features by solely retraining the fully connected layer. To address the mismatch between the distribution of training and test data, Lu et al. [32] quantified the differences between the source and target domains by reconstructing the feature vectors, which were fed into a fully connected layer for regression computation. The discrepancy between the two domains was minimized. Eventually, the MAE of SOH estimation was within 8.87 % for target battery without labels. Mondal et al. [33] proposed an adaptive transfer learning strategy based on Gaussian process regression, which maps the data in the source and target domains to a common feature space by learning a flexible kernel function for knowledge transfer in this space. The final experimental results demonstrated that the proposed method could still reliably estimate SOH under 20 % of the training data. Domain adaptation aims to minimize the performance degradation of target domain models by reducing the discrepancy between source and target domains [34]. However, insufficient sample information within a single source domain can lead to inadequate or even negative transfer in the source domain model [35]. Song et al. [36] proposed a multi-source transfer method for predicting remaining useful life. This method dynamically selects transferable samples using dynamic time warping, transferring degradation information from various samples in multiple source domains, thereby addressing the issues of negative and insufficient transfer in multi-source domains. Ye et al. [37] developed a multi-source domain adaptation method for battery capacity estimation. They reduced the discrepancy between multi-source domains and the target domain through features and distribution discrepancy alignment. An adversarial learning was introduced to generate domain-adaptive features, achieving SOH estimation with an average error of less than 1.5 %. For another semi-supervised learning paradigm addressing the sample scarcity problem, relatively few studies have been conducted in the field of SOH and RUL estimation, but semi-supervised learning has a promising future in dealing with large amounts of unlabeled data. Guo et al. [38] used a specific semi-supervised learning technique of partial Bayesian co-training to facilitate the modeling of battery life prediction by extracting information from unlabeled data, which proved to be 21.9 % better than the considered baseline in terms of battery life prediction accuracy. The proposed semi-supervised learning improved the accuracy and interpretability of the battery life prediction. Lin et al. [39] developed a semi-supervised co-estimation algorithm that pseudo-labels derived from two heterogeneous regression estimators for model training with small labeled samples. The final result of SOH estimation was improved up to 53 % over the baseline experiments without labeled data. To avoid the accumulation of errors from inaccurate pseudo-labels during joint training, Yao et al. [40] proposed a semi-supervised learning algorithm that combined an adversarial generative network and a convolutional neural network to estimate the capacity of lithium-ion batteries. The adversarial neural network enhanced the model's ability to perceive aging information through unsupervised pattern, achieving SOH estimation RMSE of 2 %. Xiang et al. [41] proposed a semi-supervised SOH estimation method based on bi-directional gate recursive units (GRU) and structured kernel interpolation Gaussian process regression, which extracted random discharge features from only 1.5 %−15 % of labeled data with a minimum RMSE of less than 1.91 %. Xiong et al. [42] proposed an end-to-end battery capacity estimation architecture using three convolutional layers to extract raw measurement features of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, combined with semi-supervised learning to facilitate features reconstruction on unlabeled data to augment a small amount of labeled data. Compared with supervised framework, the estimation error was reduced by 50.66 %.

However, SOH and RUL estimation methods based on transfer learning and semi-supervised learning still suffer from the following challenges in addressing small sample training data:
•
The generally popular recurrent neural network variants for time-series prediction have relatively weak local perception of battery aging information, and cannot simultaneously focus on the global information of the entire aging sequence.
•
In practical transfer learning applications, different batteries lead to distribution differences between source and target domain data due to various in materials, fabrication processes, and conditions, which are difficult to be eliminated, resulting in poor applicability of the source domain model in the target domain.
•
Most of the transfer learning methods used for SOH and RUL estimation are constructed based on a fully supervised learning framework. The abundant unlabeled data is neglected, and it is difficult to achieve the expected accuracy by fine-tuning the model with only a small amount of labeled data.

To address the above problems, this paper proposes an enhanced vision-transformer (ViT) integrating with semi-supervised transfer learning for SOH and RUL estimation of lithium-ion batteries. The work makes the following contributions:
(1)
A depth-wise separable convolutional vision-transformer (DeViT) is proposed, which retains the encoder of the transformer for focusing on the global aging information of batteries. A depth-wise separable convolution is introduced to focus on the local aging details, which improves the parallel computation capability and ensures the high accuracy of SOH and RUL estimation.
(2)
To achieve domain adaptation, maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) is introduced to measure the distance between two related distributions. The distance is minimized to optimize the weights of backpropagation for the proposed transformer model, aiming to reduce the inconsistency between the data distributions of the source and target domains.
(3)
Semi-supervised learning (SSL) with consistency regularization is developed to label abundant degradation data outside the source domain using limited labeled data in target domain. Consistency regularization incorporates the cross-entropy between predictions with and without adversarial perturbations into the gradient backpropagation of the overall model. Thereby, the vast unlabeled aging data is fully utilized to enhance the development of target domain model.

The rest of this research is structured as follows: the methodology of the proposed method for SOH and RUL estimation is explained in Section 2; the aging features extraction and datasets are described in Section 3; the proposed method is validated in Section 4; the conclusions are drawn in Section 5.

This section primarily delves into the theoretical underpinnings of the proposed approach. A novel vision-transformer architecture is introduced, integrating the self-attention mechanism of transformers with depth-wise separable convolutions. This fusion enables simultaneous attention to global and local battery aging information, thereby enhancing the model's estimation accuracy. When conducting transfer learning using the novel vision-transformer model, the MMD is employed to initially reduce the data distribution disparity between the source and target domains. The depth-wise separable convolutional layers of the pre-trained source domain model are frozen to preserve similar aging features among different types of batteries, while the remaining network parameters undergo fine-tuning in the target domain. Furthermore, a semi-supervised learning with consistency regularization is proposed to label data of the same battery type outside the source and target domains with high quality. A significant amount of unlabeled data is leveraged to retrain the unfrozen portions of the source domain model, ultimately achieving high-precision estimations of the target battery's SOH and RUL. The overall architecture of the proposed method is depicted in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Depth-wise separable convolutional vision-transformer for SOH and RUL estimation
ce:section.ce:para: For the sequential tasks of SOH and RUL estimation, numerous temporal prediction models based on recurrent neural networks and convolutional operations have been established, including LSTM, GRU, and CNN-GRU. However, these models rely on the output of the previous time steps for their current predictions, thereby limiting their ability to capture long-term dependencies in battery degradation sequences. As the length of sequences increases, computational efficiency becomes a significant constraint. The gradient explosion or vanishing could arise. In contrast, the transformer architecture eschews traditional recursive and convolutional operations, enabling parallel computation of long sequences about battery degradation through self-attention mechanism. This facilitates excellent global information capture across the entire sequence [43].

While the transformer excels at dynamic attention and global fusion in modeling battery degradation, it lacks the ability to effectively capture aging details and local features [44]. Visual models designed for image recognition tasks excel at processing such details. Battery degradation sequences at different stages can be analogous to color channels in an image. Therefore, this paper adopts a vision-transformer architecture, which retains the encoder of the transformer to capture global information. Patch embedding and position embedding operations are incorporated to account for local sequence components and positional information [45]. However, the simple linear projection used for attention in the transformer's encoder could lead to a loss of degradation information. Further, a depth-wise separable convolution is introduced between patch embedding and transformer encoder to enhance local information perception. Depth-wise separable convolution extracts features from different sequence positions within each degradation stage using depth-wise convolution [46]. The aging information is fused across different degradation channels through pointwise convolution. This approach significantly reduces computational complexity compared to traditional convolutions and facilitates learning correlations between features.
The complete internal working mechanism of the proposed DeViT is summarized as follows, and shown in Fig. 2 :

1) Patch embedding. The various aging feature (AF) sequences of the battery are organized into a two-dimensional matrix and transformed into a three-dimensional matrix [[AF 1, AF 2, AF 3, …, AFn ]1, [AF 1, AF 2, AF 3, …, AFn ]2, …, [AF 1, AF 2, AF 3, …, AFn ] Dim ]. The three-dimensional matrix is divided into Z patches, as shown in Eq. (1). Each patch is then flattened into Z ×(Pw ×Ph ×Dim) two-dimensional vector through the Flatten operation, as demonstrated in Eq. (2).
(1)
Z =
L f
× n
P w
×
P h
where n presents the number of AFs; Lf presents the length of the feature sequence; Pw presents the width of patch; Ph presents the height of patch; Dim presents the dimension of features.
(2)
x i
= F l a t t e n
(
x
i − 1
)
where Flatten is an operation for matrix dimensionality reduction, and xi is the patch after dividing in Eq. (1), with i = 1, 2, 3, …, N.

2) Position embedding. A class token is added to each patch after the Flatten operation for features classification. Additionally, positional encoding is incorporated at each location within each patch to assist the model in accurately capturing critical aging information, as shown in Eq. (3).
(3)
X 0
=
[
x
t o k e n
,
x 1
,
x 2
,
x 3
, . . . ,
x i
]
+ P o s i t i o n
where X 0 is the sequence used for transformer encoding and xtoken is the class token. Position is a matrix with dimension (Z+1)×(Pw ×Ph ×Lf ) to provide position information.

3) Depth-wise separable convolution. As shown in Fig. 3 , the depth-wise convolution is carried out separately on clusters of token vector matrices output from the previous step for each aging matrix. The pointwise convolution is performed on different aging channels to obtain the format that can be encoded by the transformer encoder. The output of the depth-wise separable convolution is described in Eq. (4).
(4)
x i
=
x
i − 1
+ D e p t h w i s e
(
x
i − 1
)
+ P o i n t w i s e
(
x
i − 1
)
where Depthwise denotes the depth-wise convolution; Pointwise denotes the pointwise convolution.

4) Encoding. The sequence resulting from the preceding steps is fed into the transformer encoder for encoding. The multi-head attention operates as follows: Each x
i in the sequence X 0 undergoes an embedding operation to produce [a 1, a 2, a 3, …, ai ]. Subsequently, each ai is transformed using WQ, WK , and WV matrices to derive Qi (Query), Ki (Key), and Vi (Value) as Eqs. (5)-(7). Qi, Ki , and Vi are then partitioned into multiple Qi,j, Ki,j , and Vi,j , with j = 1, 2, 3, …, N. Finally, the scaled dot product attention is applied to these matrices, yielding the output of the multi-head attention as detailed in Eqs. (8)-(10).
(5)
Q i
=
W Q
a i
(6)
K i
=
W K
a i
(7)
V i

(8)

where Q and K denote dimension vectors, and dk denotes the dimension of the vector containing Q and K; Q, K, and V denote the matrices of the set of Query, Key, and Value vectors, respectively; WQ, WK , and WV denote the projection matrices used to generate the Query, Key, and Value matrices, respectively; WO denotes the output of the multiplexed projection matrix; The expression for Softmax is
S o f t m a x

N

x i
, i = 1, 2, 3, …; Concat is used to splice multiple arrays together.

5) SOH estimation in MLP regression layer. The MLP module receives output from transformer encoder for estimating SOH, as shown in Eq. (11).
(11)
S O H = M L P (
x
t o k e n , o u t
)
where SOH denotes the result of SOH estimation; MLP is the multilayer perceptron used to make regression predictions; xtoken,out is the output sequence of transformer encoder.

6) RUL estimation. The number of cycles corresponding to the estimated SOH at the end of battery life is calculated as the estimated RUL. The relative error of RUL is calculated as Eq. (12).
(12)
RE =
| L −
L
Esti mated
|
L
where RE represents the relative error; L is the cycle number at actual SOH of 80 %; LEstimated is the cycle number at estimated SOH of 80 %.

Transfer learning delivers the designed deep learning model yielding from a task in one domain to another related task. This learning paradigm alleviates the model's requirement for extensive labeled data. The learning mainly involves two processes: source task learning and knowledge transfer. Source task learning refers to training the model on the source domain, which provides abundant labeled data for learning. Knowledge transfer involves applying the gradient weights obtained from the source domain to the target task, thus minimizing the target domain's dependency on extensive labeled data.

In the realm of lithium-ion battery SOH and RUL estimation, the data distribution of battery aging characteristics varies due to disparities in manufacturing processes, chemical structures, and operational environments. Such variations are difficult to be addressed through fine-tuning of parameters. Therefore, the MMD is utilized to measure the discrepancy between the source and target domains and guide the model's training process. The essence of MMD is to project the mean distributions of two different samples into a reproducing kernel Hilbert space, where each distribution is mapped to a point [47]. Then, a kernel function is employed to measure the similarity between different data points and minimize the distance between them. The Gaussian kernel function is chosen in this study to measure the similarity of battery aging data in transfer learning, as it effectively transforms nonlinear relationships in the original data into linear relationships in a high-dimensional feature space, making linearly inseparable data separable. The computed MMD loss is seamlessly integrated into the model's backpropagation loss, as illustrated in Eq. (13).
(13)
L MMD
=
∥
1 m
∑
i = 1

φ
(

)

)

where LMMD represents the loss of MMD; H represents the regeneration kernel Hilbert space; φ represents the mapping function of feature x to H;

represents the aging feature sequence of the ith battery in the source domain;

represents the aging feature sequence of the ith battery in the target domain; m and n represent the number of batteries in the source and target domains, respectively; K represents the Gaussian kernel function.

where σ represents the normal distribution bandwidth and is usually set to 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Semi-supervised transfer learning with consistency regularization
ce:section.ce:para:
The MMD typically assumes a certain similarity between the source and target domains, rendering it advantageous in addressing distribution differences among similar types of battery data. Nevertheless, it is often challenging to ensure that the source domain comprehensively covers various types of battery data. Significant disparities in data distribution between the source and target domains can lead to ineffective transfer learning. This paper introduces a semi-supervised transfer learning approach with consistency regularization to address the problem. In real scenarios, there exists an abundance of unlabeled aging data that is identical to the target battery type but remains largely unused. These data are assigned pseudo-labels through semi-supervised learning with consistency regularization. The MMD-based transfer learning (MMD-TL) introduced in Section 2.2 is employed to train the source domain model and initially minimize distribution differences between source and target data. Subsequently, the pseudo-labeled data obtained through semi-supervised learning is incorporated to further enhance the source domain model's comprehension of battery aging specifics pertinent to the target domain. The accurate estimation of the target battery's SOH and RUL is achieved. The principle of semi-supervised learning with consistency regularization is as follows: A well-designed model exhibits stable output distributions after an adequate training [48]. Upon introducing a slight perturbation radv to the input dataset xl , the output distribution q(Gf (xl +radv )|xl +radv ) remains largely unchanged. In other words, the stability of the model is judged by the predicted output distribution, and the difference between the perturbed output distribution q(Gf (xl +radv )|xl +radv ) and the unperturbed output distribution q(Gf (xl )|xl ) is quantified by cross-entropy. The cross-entropy serves as the consistency regularization loss, which participates in the backpropagation and updates the overall model weights, guiding the model towards a more stable output. A smaller cross-entropy value indicates a more stable model and higher-quality pseudo-labels. The slight perturbation introduced to the input dataset can be regarded as adversarial noise on a prior distribution. The order of magnitude of slight perturbation can be defined as one-tenth of the input data. The loss calculation formulas for semi-supervised learning with consistency regularization, regression prediction, and the overall model proposed in this paper are provided in Eqs. (15)-(17), respectively. The pseudo-code for the proposed semi-supervised transfer learning with consistency regularization is provided in Table 1 .
(15)
L

)

)

a d v
)
]

)
· q

)
)

Lcons represents the consistency regularization loss; D represents the metric function between the two distributions, the cross-entropy function is used in this study; q and p represent the true and perturbed output label distributions, respectively; Gf is the proposed regression model for SOH estimation; xl denotes an unlabeled battery aging feature sequence; radv represents simulated random noise perturbations, which follows a standard normal distribution and is set 0.1 here.
(16)
L
M S E
=
1 n
∑
j = 1
n
(
G f
(
x j
)
− y
(
x j
)
)
2
where LMSE denotes the loss of mean square error resulting from training the proposed model with labeled data; y represents the true label corresponding to xj .
(17)
L
T o t a l
= λ
L
M S E
+ β
L
M M D
+ ψ
L
C O N S
where LTotal denotes the total loss calculated of the proposed model trained in target domain. λ, β, and ψ are weight coefficients for various loss terms, with their sum equaling 1. In validation with the same type of batteries, λ, β, and ψ were set to 0.9, 0.1, and 0, respectively. In validating with different types of batteries, the greater reliance on the loss from SSL is needed. λ, β, and ψ were adjusted to 0.4, 0.1, and 0.5, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery datasets description and features extraction

ce:section.ce:para: The dataset from National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) consists of cylindrical 18650-LiNiCoAlO₂ batteries. B5, B6, and B7 are selected for this study, with rated capacities of 1860 mAh, 2040 mAh, and 1890 mAh, respectively. The batteries were tested at 24 °C. Charging was performed with constant current of 1.5 A and constant voltage of 4.2 V. The cut-off current was 20 mA. Discharge was conducted at a constant current of 2 A until the cut-off voltage was reached [49].

The dataset from Center for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering (CALCE) contains prismatic LiCoO2 batteries. In particular, CS2_35, CS2_36, and CS2_37 batteries are chosen for analysis, with a rated capacity of 1100 mAh. These batteries were charged using a standard protocol of constant current (0.55 A) and constant voltage (4.2 V), with a cut-off current of 50 mA. Discharge was performed at a constant current of 1.1 A until the cut-off voltage is reached [50].

The dataset from University of Oxford includes pouch-type LiCoO2 batteries. In this paper, Cell1, Cell2, and Cell3 are studied, with a rated capacity of 740 mAh. Battery testing was conducted at 40 °C. Charging was carried out with a constant current of 0.74 A, followed by discharging under the dynamic operating condition, specifically referring to the Artemis urban driving cycle (AUDC). Battery data was sampled every 100 cycle periods [51].

The laboratory batteries, Lab1 and Lab2, are cylindrical 18650 Li(NiCoMn)O2 batteries with a rated capacity of 3 Ah. To simulate real-world conditions, both batteries were charged at 2C rate to an upper cutoff voltage of 4.2 V and a cutoff current of 0.15 A. After a brief resting period, the batteries were discharged under the urban dynamometer driving schedule (UDDS) to a lower cutoff voltage of 2.5 V. The testing temperature was 25 °C, and the batteries underwent cyclic aging until they reached the end-of-life. The details of all battery datasets used in this paper are presented in Table 2 .

In this paper, aging features are extracted during the battery charging phase, which is more stable in practical scenarios. The extraction of battery aging features is conducted from two perspectives: the battery's capacity storage ability and energy storage capacity at different aging stages. Firstly, the area enclosed by the IC curve and the coordinate axis is extracted as a representation of the battery's capacity storage ability. The IC curve is smoothed using the moving average method. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the peak area of the IC curve continuously decreases with battery aging, reflecting the loss of lithium ions and negative electrode materials. The shift of the IC curve towards higher voltages indicates an increase in the battery's internal impedance. Additionally, the integral of the measured voltage and time during the charging process is easily obtained and indirectly reflects the battery's energy storage capacity at different aging stages. Therefore, the area enclosed by the measured voltage and time coordinates during charging is also extracted as an aging feature, as shown in Fig. 4b. Furthermore, charging duration and cycle number are also selected. The charging duration under the same charging protocol directly reflects the battery's capacity, and the cycle number is commonly used to indicate battery lifespan. Notably, the aforementioned aging features are extracted from partial SOC intervals for SOH and RUL estimation, which aligns better with practical applications.

The material, manufacturing process, packaging type, test environment temperature, and operating conditions of batteries can all lead to inconsistencies between source domain models and target domain models. Therefore, this article defines batteries with the same cathode and anode materials as the same type of batteries, while the remaining batteries are defined as the different types. To explore the adaptability of the proposed model across both types of batteries, this paper groups the four datasets for cross-validation. Ablation tests are gradually conducted on each experimental group to verify the effectiveness of the proposed strategies and models. The grouping is shown in Table 3 .

The superiority of the proposed DeViT model and extracted features is validated on the B5, B6, and B7 battery datasets from NASA. The B5, B6, and B7 batteries belong to the same type. The source domain datasets are created by pairing any two of these three batteries, while the third battery serves as the target battery for testing. The participating models are trained in the source domain and partially frozen to preserve typical features of battery aging similar to the target battery. After migrating the source domain model to the target domain, the first 25 % of the target battery's data is used for fine-tuning. An MMD loss term is added to the migrated model to compensate for the data distribution differences between the source and target domains caused by battery manufacturing processes, thus completing the construction of the target domain model.

The CS2_35, CS2_36, and CS2_37 batteries from CALCE and Cell1, Cell2, and Cell3 batteries from Oxford belong to the same type of batteries. The source domain datasets are constructed using battery data from CALCE and Oxford. The target batteries are the B5, B6, and B7 batteries from NASA. Additionally, NASA's battery data serves as the training set for estimating the SOH of target batteries from CALCE and Oxford in the source domain model. Similarly, B5, CS2_35, and Cell1 constitute the source domain dataset, while Lab1 and Lab2 in the laboratory serve as the target batteries, respectively. These experimental groups are designed to validate the applicability of the proposed method across different battery types. It is noteworthy that only one battery of each type is utilized for validation in the target domain. Meanwhile, the SOH of the remaining two batteries remains unlabeled, simulating the abundance of unlabeled data encountered in industrial settings for batteries of similar types. The limited labeled data of the target battery is used to pseudo-label the SOH of the other two batteries through the proposed semi-supervised learning with consistency regularization, and these pseudo-labels are added to the fine-tuning training of the target domain model.

The performance of the proposed model is evaluated using MAE, RMSE, standard deviation of absolute error (SDE), and the coefficient of determination (R2), which can be expressed as:
(18)

where,
H ^

presents the estimated value, Hi presents the reference value,
H ¯
i
presents the mean value of the reference quantity, and n presents the number of units.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of MMD-based transfer learning for the same type of batteries

ce:section.ce:para: The proposed DeViT model was evaluated on the NASA dataset, benchmarked against commonly used recurrent neural networks like LSTM, GRU, and CNN-GRU. These models are prevalent in battery SOH and RUL estimation. The MMD-TL was further combined with the proposed DeViT and the recurrent neural networks to reduce the distribution differences in aging data. Additionally, the ViT was included in the comparison to underscore the advancements of the proposed model. The hidden layers of recurrent neural networks typically contain generic representations of features learned by the model. The fully connection layers are responsible for transforming the features from the hidden layers into task-specific outputs. Therefore, the weights of the hidden layers in LSTM, GRU, and CNN-GRU were frozen after training in source domain. The full connection layers were fine-tuned in the target domain. In the proposed DeViT model, the transformer's encoder utilizes multi-head attention to establish global dependencies among different levels of battery degradation. Consequently, the weights of the multi-head attention were frozen, and the weights of the depth-wise separable convolution were adjusted in the source domain to capture aging details specific to the target battery.

The adjustment range of batch size for the compared models in this study ranged from 8 to 512, adjusted in increments of powers of 2. The epochs started from 30 and increased by 5 units until there was no significant decrease in the training loss. Ultimately, the batch size was set to 128, and the epoch was set to 80 for all compared models. GRU and LSTM had similar architectures with 64 hidden units. Specifically, the CNN-GRU featured 32 convolutional kernels, with a kernel size of 1 × 1 and a stride of 1. For the common structure of DeViT and ViT, the patch size was 2 × 2, with a stride of 2. The attention heads number of transformer encoder was 8. The MLP had 3 perception layers. DeViT additionally had 3 × 3 depth-wise and 1 × 1 pointwise convolutions, both with stride and padding of 1. All compared models utilized the Adam optimizer, with a learning rate of 0.0001 in source domain. The dropout was 0.1. The loss function for all models was mean squared error. The models were implemented in a Python 3.7 based PyTorch 1.1.0 virtual environment, with AMD Ryzen 9 5900X and NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 hardware.

Experimental groups 1, 2, and 3 explored the model comparison results using B7, B6, and B5 as the target batteries, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5 and Table 4 . In experimental group 1, the proposed DeViT exhibited the best performance, with MAE, RMSE, and SDE less than 0.5 % and R2 exceeding 0.99. Compared to ViT, the maximum improvement in accuracy across these four metrics was 31.34 %. GRU and LSTM failed to perceive local aging details, resulting in RMSE exceeding 1 % for SOH estimation. The estimation results of experimental group 2 were similar to those of group 1. Although the stability of the proposed DeViT decreased slightly, its regression capability for SOH estimation remained robust. The CNN-GRU maintained an MAE for SOH estimation around 1 %, demonstrating the effectiveness of the extracted aging features. The MAE and RMSE of LSTM exceeded 2 %, possibly indicating that LSTM fell into a local optimal solution during training in the source domain and could not generalize well to the target domain. In experimental group 3, all models exhibited stable performance. Except for LSTM, the MAE for SOH estimation of the other four models was within 1 %. From another perspective, the proposed approach of utilizing MMD-based transfer learning to address data distribution inconsistencies between source and target domains of similar batteries is feasible. Moreover, the superiority of the proposed DeViT model was fully utilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of semi-supervised transfer learning with consistency regularization for different types of batteries
Validation results of different battery experimental groups
ce:section.ce:para: Different types of batteries exhibit variations in internal chemical degradation patterns and phase transition processes due to differences in material properties. When the source domain lacks data of the target battery type, simply fine-tuning partial layers of a frozen model is inadequate to address inconsistencies in data distribution across battery types. This section introduces semi-supervised learning with consistency regularization. A significant portion of unlabeled battery data, similar to the target battery type and extending beyond the source and target domains, is labeled using 25 % labeled data from the target battery. This data is then used in the fine-tuning of the target domain model to reduce the difference between the source and target domains. Fourteen sets of validation experiments are conducted in this section following the groups 4–17 described in Section 4.1.

Fig. 6 and Table 5 present the validation results with source domain batteries originating from the CALCE dataset and target domain batteries from the NASA dataset. Compared to the sole approaches of MMD-TL and SSL, the proposed model demonstrates superior agreement to the reference values. The MMD-TL fails to bridge the data distribution gap effectively between the source and target domains, thereby restraining the estimation capabilities of the DeViT model. Consequently, estimation results of MMD-TL for NASA batteries are significantly inferior, offering limited predictions of aging trajectories that fall short of expectations. In contrast, SSL significantly outperforms MMD-TL in the estimation. Despite utilizing the same 25 % labeled target battery data as MMD-TL, SSL enhances DeViT's generalization capabilities via consistency regularization. The MAE and RMSE of SSL estimation in experimental groups 4–6 did not exceed 1.7 % and 2 %, respectively. Notably, SSL's R2 values exceeded 0.97, indicating excellent regression performance. These results further underscore SSL's ability to accurately label a large amount of unlabeled data beyond the source and target domains. The proposed model exhibits optimal performance across various estimation metrics. For batteries B5 and B6, the SOH estimation MAE was less than 0.9 %, and the RMSE was less than 1.3 %. Compared to SSL and MMD-TL, the proposed model shows the best performance.

When the source domain batteries are from Oxford and the target batteries are from NASA, Fig. 7 and Table 6 provide a comparison of different models. The estimation results of MMD-TL remain similar to those in the previous scenario. The SSL further demonstrates robustness in scenarios with limited labeled data. The proposed model maintains its superiority across all evaluation metrics for batteries B5 and B6. Specifically, for battery B5, the SOH estimation MAE was less than 0.5 %, while RMSE and SDE were both less than 0.8 %, and R2 exceeded 0.99. However, the estimation stability of the proposed model on battery B6 was slightly compromised due to the inaccurate target domain modeling of MMD-TL, which yielded the R2 of only 0.6120 for this battery.

In experimental groups 10–12, the NASA dataset was used to train the source domain model, and the CALCE dataset was employed for validation of the target domain model. The validation results are presented in Fig. 8 and Table 7 . Overall, the estimation performance on the CALCE dataset significantly improved compared to that in experimental groups 4–9. The MMD-TL achieved certain effectiveness across different types of batteries through MMD and fine-tuning of target domain parameters, with MAE of SOH estimation less than 2 %. Compared to SSL, the proposed model reduced the MAE and RMSE of SOH estimation for the CS2_35 battery by 56.39 % and 60.76 %, respectively. Although the stability of the proposed model's estimation for the CS2_36 battery slightly decreased due to the influence of consistency regularization, the estimation error remained at around 1 %.

In experimental groups 13–15, a comparative analysis was conducted on the source and target domain datasets comprising of NASA and Oxford datasets. The validation results are presented in Fig. 9 and Table 8 . The proposed model exhibited the best estimation performance among all control groups for the Oxford dataset. Specifically, the MAE, RMSE, and SDE for SOH estimation of the target batteries Cell1, Cell2, and Cell3 were all less than 0.7 %, with R2 exceeding 0.99. Notably, for Cell3 battery, the MAE for SOH estimation was merely 0.32 %, and the RMSE was only 0.46 %. Besides the proposed model, SSL and MMD-TL also achieved impressive results, contributing to the accurate acquisition of aging information by the proposed model. The relatively small size of labeled sample and less pronounced capacity growth in the Oxford dataset reduced difficulty of the estimation.

The target batteries in groups 16 and 17 were tested in the laboratory, with the results presented in Fig. 10 and Table 9 . The proposed model exhibited remarkable accuracy in estimating the SOH of the target battery Lab1, with MAE, RMSE, SDE less than 0.5 %, and R2 exceeding 0.98. These results were superior to both SSL and MMD-TL. Due to the exacerbated nonlinear aging of batteries under dynamic operating conditions, the estimation performance of the proposed model on the target battery Lab2 slightly decreased. However, both MAE and RMSE of battery Lab2 remained within 1 %. The generalization of the proposed method was further validated.

Additionally, the RUL estimation results for the experimental groups consisting of public datasets are summarized in Table 10 . The maximum relative error of RUL estimation stood at 6.98 %, signifying that the proposed model maintains satisfactory performance in predicting battery lifespan. In particular, under the complex dynamic operating conditions at 40 °C, the mean relative error of RUL estimation for the three experimental groups was 3.86%. The fourteen controlled experiments comprehensively validated the proposed model and showcased its interpretability. The model effectively captured similar degradation trajectories among different types of batteries based on the source domain model, while leveraging a significant amount of aging data from the same battery type as the target with the assistance of SSL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation results with different labeling rates in target domain
ce:section.ce:para: The above discussion has focused on the generalization and estimation accuracy of the proposed method across various battery material types, packaging forms, operating conditions, and capacities. The impact of different labeling rates on the overall model's estimation accuracy and stability is further analyzed in this section. Specifically, one set of experimental data was selected to label battery data in the target domain at varying proportions. Experimental group 4 was used for analysis, with five scenarios tested: 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, and 90 % labeling rates for the target battery data. As shown in Table 11 and Fig. 11 , the SOH estimation results indicate a general decrease in estimation error with increasing labeling quantities. Notably, when the labeling rate exceeded 30 %, the MAEs of SOH estimation were less than 1 %. Even with a mere 10 % labeling rate for the target battery, the estimation error of the proposed model did not exceed 1.5 %, which holds significant practical implications. From a stability perspective, when the target battery labeling rate was below 50 %, the RMSEs of SOH estimation remained within 1.5 %, suggesting that the model could maintain a concentrated error distribution and strong stability even with low labeling rates. Moreover, as the labeling rate decreased from 90 % to 10 %, the R2 value remained above 0.95, which indicated the model's excellent fitting capability for complex nonlinear systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation results within different SOC windows
ce:section.ce:para: This study further explores the sensitivity of the proposed model to input data within different SOC windows. The SOC windows range from 10 % to 90 %, with a window width of 40 % SOC and a moving step of 10 % SOC. Ultimately, partial SOC intervals of 10 %∼50 %, 20 %∼60 %, 30 %∼70 %, 40 %∼80 %, and 50 %∼90 % are utilized for demonstration and analysis, covering varying charging ranges of the batteries. As shown in Table 12 and Fig. 12 , the proposed method achieves promising results in the experimental group 5, with MAEs of SOH estimation less than 1.2 %. Additionally, the RMSEs were less than 1.7 %, further demonstrating the robustness of the model. However, the model exhibits varying sensitivity to data within different SOC intervals. Specifically, when the starting SOC of the window is 20 %, 30 %, and 40 %, the model demonstrates similar sensitivity in terms of estimation accuracy and stability. These three ranges encompass the peak region of the IC curve, which essentially reflects the lithium storage of the battery and the loss of active materials. The 20 %∼60 % SOC range aligns best with the model, achieving the minimum MAE of 0.75 %. Conversely, the SOH estimation results in the 10 %∼50 % and 50 %∼90 % SOC ranges are relatively inferior due to the omission of the IC curve peak, yet the R2 values remain above 0.98. Therefore, the proposed method exhibits excellent adaptability to different charging segments, promising significant potential for practical advancement.

To mitigate the dependence of lithium-ion battery SOH and RUL estimation on abundant labeled data and the inconsistency in battery data feature distribution, this paper proposes an enhanced vision-transformer integrating with semi-supervised transfer learning for SOH and RUL estimation. The enhanced vision-transformer is developed by embedding depth-wise separable convolution to capture local information of battery aging. Multi-head attention is utilized to establish global dependency among different aging sequences. Maximum mean discrepancy is combined with transfer learning to initially mitigate the distribution discrepancy between aging data in the source and target domain, providing a better starting point for fine-tuning the target domain model. Furthermore, when the source and target domain datasets come from different types of batteries, semi-supervised learning with consistency regularization is introduced to label numerous battery data of the same type as the target battery. The labeled data is then injected into the target domain to facilitate fine-tuning of the target domain model. Consistency regularization incorporates the cross-entropy between predictions with and without adversarial perturbations into the gradient backpropagation update of the overall model. SOH and RUL estimation validations were performed based on the enhanced vision-transformer model combined with MMD-transfer learning among batteries of the same type. Compared with vision-transformer, convolutional neural networks-gated recurrent units, gate recursive units, and long short-term memory, the proposed model performed the best. The maximum MAE of SOH estimation did not exceed 0.6 %, and the maximum relative error of RUL estimation did not exceed 3 %. Subsequently, further validations were conducted for SOH and RUL estimations across different types of batteries. A large amount of unlabeled aging data was fully utilized through semi-supervised learning. The MAE of SOH estimation for the target battery was less than 1.39 %, while the maximum relative error of RUL estimation stood at 6.98 %. To explore the limitations of the proposed method, the impact of varying labeling rates and SOC windows was further discussed. Despite a labeling rate of only 10 %, the RMSE of the SOH estimation remained within 1.5 %. Moreover, the proposed method exhibited significant generalization across different SOC windows, with a maximum RMSE not exceeding 1.7 %. In future work, reducing model complexity and computational burden will be considered.

Ya-Xiong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shangyu Zhao: Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shiquan Wang: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Kai Ou: Software, Project administration. Jiujun Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Major Project of Fujian Province of China (Grant No. 2022HZ028018), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51907030).
